title: WOMB WITH A VIEW
text: WOMB WITH A VIEW by Chad Lutzke S tan Lipton, MD stood on the roof of the hospital in blood-smeared scrubs, teetering on the edge. He’d been up here plenty of times before, mostly to help when a patient was airlifted in, sometimes to sneak a cigarette between surgeries. But never to contemplate jumping. The roof had become a place to look for answers, to search the stars for signs of something more. Maybe the emptiness he felt came with the job, where the daily reminder of mortality clawed at him and the traumatic screams of victim’s families filled his dreams. Maybe it was just a matter of quitting the job to regain the tranquility that seemed to erode like a patinated graveyard statue no one came to visit. Or perhaps the damage had been done, the scars too deep to run from. He looked down at the street and pictured the mess he’d leave when landing, the mortified witnesses who would see the spattering of blood, like the art project in school when he pushed the watery ink with his breath through a straw. But this wouldn’t be art. Art lasts forever. He took a step back, off the narrow ledge. Not because he didn’t have it in him, but because of those traumatized few. The nightmares he’d cause those cursed with laying eyes on snapped bone through skin, hearing the smack of skull on pavement, people like Mr. Jane at the newsstand, who always made sure a copy of the latest issue of Popular Mechanics was hidden safely behind the racks so Stan was sure to get one after clocking out from a 14-hour shift. Or the elderly woman who walked her cat along 32nd street, stopping for a hot dog around dinner time. And what of the children who frequented the arcade down the street, sneaking cigarettes from their parents and spraying each other with cologne to mask the scent before going home. It would change them all. He flicked his spent cigarette on the ground. Fireworks. Then opened the door and headed inside to finish his shift. It was the least he could do. # Stan rushed past the breakroom, but it was too late. Greg had seen him. “What’s up, Stan the man?” Stan slowed, didn’t roll his eyes at the overused pet name. “Hey, Greg.” “Wait up.” Greg grabbed his cup of coffee and ran to the hall. “Rebecca was looking for you. Hamilton didn’t show, and Weston’s delivering already. You mind taking over a delivery?” “Right now?” “Yeah. They’ve induced, and she’s at seven centimeters.” Stan shook his head at the irony of bringing in life while on the verge of ending his own. “You know I haven’t delivered in years, right?” “It’s like riding a bike.” Greg joked. Stan hesitated, then thought of the patient in room 201 who had yet to wake up and realize he’d lost a leg. He didn’t want to be there when it happened. “Yeah, okay. But you’ve gotta take my rounds.” “Done.” Stan washed, suited up, and had a short discussion with himself in the mirror about keeping his shit together, going through the steps of childbirth. He’d spent so many days among the dead and dying in the past several years that delivering a newborn felt as foreign as taking a boat down the Amazon with local natives. Finish the shift. Then you can go home and do it. Go out knowing you brought something in . He snapped his gloves and pushed elbows through the washroom doors. The nurses stood waiting. There was no father present. No partner at all to help the pregnant woman in her time of need, only the beeping of machines and the probing of stranger’s fingers. One nurse was doing her best to console the woman, who was clearly in pain, while another nurse filled the doctor in with a very unexpected turn of events. “The baby is breech,” she said. “Breech? Nobody told me that. And there’s no mention of it on her—” “It just became breech,” the nurse kept her voice at a whisper, with her back to the woman in labor. “What do you mean, it just became breech?” “I mean, I felt the baby’s head. The mother was dilated up to 9, and when I checked again, there’s his feet.” “So he’s in there doing somersaults?” The nurse shrugged, and Stan made his way to his patient. “Hello, Ms. Salyer. I’m Dr. Lipton. It looks like today’s going to be a very special day for you.” The woman, shiny with sweat, looked at each face in the room, reading them. If this were poker, they’d all lose. “What’s wrong? Something’s wrong, isn’t it?” Stan placed a hand on the woman’s arm. “Nothing we’re not prepared to handle. That’s the good news. The more interesting news is that your little guy has decided to change positions at the last minute.” “Change positions?” she said through ghost-white lips. “Instead of the baby’s head greeting us first, he wants to hit the ground running...feet first.” “He’s breech?” A pained grimace stretched her face as she held her bulging abdomen. “Why didn’t my doctor tell me? That’s not what the ultrasound showed.” “That’s because your baby wasn’t breech then. Somehow he managed to do this in the past hour. Now, do you feel any discomfort outside of the norm? Particularly in the last half hour?” “I feel a whole hell of a lot of discomfort.” The woman winced, squeezing her abdomen. “Okay. How do you feel about a Cesarean birth? There’s a chance we may have to do it anyway, especially since you’ve already been induced, but I think it’s a good idea to start heading in that direction.” Through gritted teeth, the woman said, “That’s fine...whatever it takes.” “Okay, we’re going to get an anesthesiologist in here for you. In the meantime, the nurse is going to prep you, and we’ll insert a catheter just to keep your bladder empty. If you have any questions, Angela here can answer them for you. “ Stan left and went straight for the restroom, where he studied himself in the mirror again as the faucet ran cold water. He held his hand out flat, checked the steadiness, anticipating a tremble, but it was still and level. He looked back to the mirror. “That baby wants nothing to do with this world.” His voice was a stone sailing down an empty well. He zoned out and let a montage of bad memories play on the wall before him—the divorce and all its extra-marital activity, the hundred times he’s delivered bad news to waiting families, the smoking habit he knows damn well he should kick. Several minutes went by before the opening of doors brought him back to reality. “We’re ready, Dr. Lipton,” a nurse said. He washed his hands again, then pushed through the doors, ready to force a human into this shithole. # “I don’t suppose the baby changed his mind,” Dr. Lipton said to the nurse. “Sir?” “Did he flip back around?” “No, Doctor. I don’t think he could have—” “Well, he couldn’t have done what he already did, could he?” The nurse kept silent and seemed to cower. While the mother was given oxygen, Stan made a horizontal incision above the pubic bone. A team of hands kept the area free of blood, stretching the muscle once he’d reached it. Through uneducated eyes, the procedure was brutal, even careless, as the many fingers tugged and stretched the bright red flesh. The opening was kept wide with retractors and fingers, like so many tentacles, and Stan punctured the amniotic sac, destroying the last bit of border between the baby and the world. He pushed his hand through the thick wall of flesh, then gently, but firmly, grabbed both of the baby’s feet. Gloved fingers tugged and widened the opening, making room for the tiny body, while Dr. Lipton led the baby out of its mother. Then stopped. The ease of progress through the slick path came to a halt, as though the baby was no longer cooperating. The doctor pulled again. “Give me some light...right down in there.” One quick moment later, a bright light shined inside the woman. With a determined grip, the baby was holding strong to the far end of its umbilical cord near the placenta. He wants nothing to do with this world. The doctor tugged again, gently. He prefers it inside. “Get what you can of the cord and clamp it. We need to cut early.” “What’s going on? Is my baby okay?” the mother asked. “Absolutely, Ms. Salyer. We’ve got this.” Hands grabbed the umbilical cord, carefully pulled, then clamped it. The doctor pulled again, but the baby’s insistent grip remained. It’s better in there. Another pull. It’s safe. It’s secure. It’s judgment free and quiet. “Nurse, hold the legs. I need to find out what’s going on in there.” The nurse held the baby’s legs, while Dr. Lipton slipped his arm through the incision up to the elbow. The mother attempted to prop herself up and look beyond the blue drape. “What do you mean? What’s wrong with my baby?” “Ma’am, everything is okay.” Another nurse said. “Just relax. Not every birth is the same.” If the mother could see their faces, she would accurately read confusion. “I need more light,” Dr. Lipton said. The nurse struggled with a pen light, but there were too many arms, too many fingers. “Let me see it.” The doctor held his free hand out. “And place a retractor here.” There was a disturbing urgency in his voice. “Dr. Lipton, perhaps we should just just pull the baby free. I’m sure he’ll—” “He doesn’t want to join us...I need to know why. Now put the retractor in, and give me some room.” The nurses traded glances of unsurety, while the worried mother closed her eyes and mouthed a prayer. Stan’s brow furrowed as he searched the womb, feeling for the baby’s hands, fingering the spongy walls inside. Finally, he carefully pried tiny fingers from the umbilical cord. “Pull,” he commanded. The nurse holding the legs scooped her hand under the baby and carefully pulled. The doctor slid his arm out, and with it the baby’s head. An air of relief filled the room, and the nurse covered the baby with a cloth, wiped it down, and laid it on the mother’s chest. “He’s perfect,” the nurse said. “Yes, he is.” The mother’s sweat-filled face stretched into an impossibly wide smile, as she held her newborn. “Congratulations. I’ll give you two a moment, and then we’ll need to cut the cord closer and get him checked out and cleaned up. In the meantime, the doctor is going to retrieve the placenta, so you should feel a little pressure.” Dr. Lipton stared at the gaping hole in the woman’s abdomen. There was a secret inside that hole, a reason for living, something that offered enlightenment. Like a junkie with its needle, the baby had to be pried from it. The nurse shifted the retractor slightly, a gentle nudge for the doctor to please carry on with the procedure. Stan pushed passed the red flappy doorway with his fingers and drove both hands into the empty chamber, where the baby held its secrets for nine months. He trailed the remainder of the cord back to the placenta, gripped it, and pulled it out, perhaps too quickly, too rough. But his mission wasn’t to finish the Cesarean procedure. It was to inspect the squishy mass, and if no answer was found there, to explore the hole further. He wanted to stay inside. The nurse watched Dr. Lipton as he squeezed the placenta, probing it with his fingers, as though searching for a pearl hidden within. “Dr. Lipton?” “Yes...give me a moment.” He searched for another few seconds, set the placenta aside, then headed back to the bloody hole. Once again, he placed his hand inside the woman, running his fingers across the baby’s vacant home. “There’s something in here,” he whispered. Oblivious to the doctor’s intentions, she waited patiently next to him, ready to assist. But when he brought his face surprisingly close to the open incision, she spoke up. “Doctor. Is everything alright?” “I plan to find out,” he said, then opened the incision wide and buried his face inside, breathing deep from the space the baby wanted to remain. “Dr. Lipton. What are you doing?” The nurse pulled at his shoulder, but he resisted, burying his face deeper still. The mother called out from the other side of the drape. “Please tell me what’s happening. I feel like this whole time you haven’t been keeping me—” She let out a groan. “Is it supposed to feel like this?” The other nurse stayed at her side, assuring her everything was fine, while wide eyes stayed glued to the doctor and his inappropriate quest for truth. “Dr. Lipton!” The nurse tugged hard at his shoulders, while half of his head was submerged. “Angela, get security.” “Security? Why?” the mother asked, holding her baby tight. Angela ran from the room while the other nurse grabbed Dr. Lipton by the hair and yanked his head back. She gasped at the unexpected look of satisfaction on the doctor’s glistening red face—saucer-wide eyes and a toothy grin. “Of course,” he said. “It’s clear to any of us from day one, but...we forget. Over the years...we forget.” Two security guards, sporting belts full of items they never get to use, grabbed the doctor and rushed him away through the doors. “We’ve known it all along,” the doctor said. “But we forget.” As the doctor was pulled through the hall, collecting stares along the way, his suicidal ideation disintegrated, replaced by contentment. And despite the trouble he was in, the grin remained, while remembering the secret we’d forgotten so many years ago. Too often in the horror genre, young writers are compared to Stephen King, with little or no basis for such a comparison. It seems like it's simply the trendy thing to do for reviewers to cry “Behold the next Stephen King!” (Note: Editor's tongue is firmly in-cheek). However, when I read Todd Keisling's epic small-town horror novel Devil's Creek , I couldn't stop thinking how reminiscent it was of King's Salem's Lot. And not because it's about vampires (it isn't) or because Todd was attempting to clone King's storytelling style (he doesn't). What he does manage, however, is to tell an epic story about a cosmic battle between good and evil while also portraying sweeping snapshots and vignettes of everyday life in this small town, and all the mundane evils which hide therein. And he does it with the same creeping ease (or unease?) he channels with “Midnight in the Southland.”
